what_it_measures: the amount of hemoglobin, an iron-rich protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen from the lungs to the rest of the body
low_meaning: low hemoglobin commonly suggests anemia, although other blood disorders or medical conditions can also contribute
high_meaning: high hemoglobin may occur with dehydration or conditions that increase red blood cell production; smoking and living at high altitude can also contribute
dietary_note: if iron deficiency is the cause of low hemoglobin, iron-rich foods together with vitamin C may help improve iron absorption; dietary changes should be based on the underlying cause
source: MedlinePlus Medical Test: Hemoglobin Test (Updated Oct 27, 2024)

what_it_measures: the total number of white blood cells, which help the body fight infection and respond to inflammation
low_meaning: a low white blood cell count may be associated with certain viral infections, medications, autoimmune disorders, or bone marrow conditions
high_meaning: a high white blood cell count may suggest infection, inflammation, physical stress, or, less commonly, certain blood disorders
dietary_note: no specific food reliably changes the white blood cell count; a balanced diet supports overall immune health
source: MedlinePlus: Complete Blood Count (CBC) / CBC Blood Test (Updated Oct 2024)

what_it_measures: the number of platelets, the blood cell fragments that help form clots and reduce bleeding
low_meaning: a low platelet count may increase the risk of bruising or bleeding and can have many possible causes
high_meaning: a high platelet count may occur in response to infection, inflammation, iron deficiency, or, less commonly, bone marrow disorders
dietary_note: there is no specific diet proven to directly raise or lower platelet counts; maintaining good overall nutrition is generally recommended
source: MedlinePlus: Complete Blood Count (CBC) / Blood Count Tests (Updated Oct 2024)

what_it_measures: the percentage of your blood volume made up of red blood cells
low_meaning: a low hematocrit may indicate anemia, blood loss, or other conditions affecting red blood cells
high_meaning: a high hematocrit may occur with dehydration or conditions that increase red blood cell production
dietary_note: adequate hydration is important, and if anemia is due to iron deficiency, iron-rich foods may be recommended
source: MedlinePlus Medical Test: Hematocrit Test (Updated Nov 25, 2024)

what_it_measures: the average size of your red blood cells (Mean Corpuscular Volume)
low_meaning: a low MCV means red blood cells are smaller than average and is commonly seen with iron deficiency anemia or certain inherited blood disorders
high_meaning: a high MCV means red blood cells are larger than average and may be associated with vitamin B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, liver disease, alcohol use, or certain medications
dietary_note: when nutritional deficiency is the cause, iron, vitamin B12, or folate intake may be important depending on the underlying diagnosis
source: MedlinePlus: Complete Blood Count (CBC) and CBC Blood Test (Updated Oct 2024)
